title: [cross-file] lichen_l2_enable disable path clears peer_table but not during stop-only
description:
Interaction: lichen_l2.c:lichen_l2_enable <-> lora_l2.c:lichen_lora_l2_stop

Bug: When lichen_l2_enable(false) is called:
1. Line 1101: lichen_lora_l2_stop() is called
2. If stop returns -ECANCELED (line 1108), deinit is called
3. Lines 1172-1176: peer_table is cleared with secure_zero

But if lichen_lora_l2_stop() is called directly (not via enable), peer_table is NOT cleared. The lora_l2 module has no knowledge of peer_table (it's in lichen_l2.c).

This creates inconsistency:
- After enable(false): peer_table cleared, replay_table cleared
- After stop() alone: peer_table preserved, replay_table preserved

If application calls stop() + start() without going through enable(), peer_table retains stale entries. This might be intentional (avoid re-EDHOC on stop/start), but it's asymmetric with the enable/disable path.

The real issue: main.c standalone mode (lines 127-128) calls lichen_lora_l2_stop() and lichen_lora_l2_deinit() directly. These don't clear peer_table. If the application tries to restart later, peer_table has stale entries.

Actually, in standalone mode there's no lichen_l2 layer at all (CONFIG_LICHEN_L2 not defined), so peer_table doesn't exist. False positive for that case.

But in CONFIG_LICHEN_L2 mode, if application calls stop/deinit/init/start without going through enable/disable, peer_table persists incorrectly.
close_reason: By design: stop()/start() intentionally preserves peer_table to allow quick restarts without re-EDHOC. The enable(false) path clears it because disable implies a full reset. The issue notes standalone mode is a false positive. In CONFIG_LICHEN_L2 mode, the asymmetry is intentional behavior.
